steps.md: **Ship a page in 90 seconds.** Pick a prompt below and run it — you'll watch Claude Code explore the workspace, plan, edit files, and verify, ending with a live personal page in the preview pane.

suggestions.text: make a page about me, in index.html
make a personal landing page about me, single index.html file, inline CSS
make a personal landing page for my photography, single index.html file, inline CSS

vague.expectedPrompt: make a page about me, in index.html

constrained.expectedPrompt: make a personal landing page about me, single index.html file, inline CSS

plan-mode.expectedPrompt: make a personal landing page for my photography, single index.html file, inline CSS

steps.md: This is the moment Claude Code goes from talking to acting: the Write tool creates `index.html` in one shot because the prompt already pinned down the file name and styling constraints.
**Why did it do that?** This replays Lesson 1's run one beat at a time. At each pause, read the annotation: which tool ran, why, and what entered the context window.

suggestions.text: make a personal landing page about me, single index.html file, inline CSS
suggestions.description: replay Lesson 1's run step by step

walkthrough.expectedPrompt: make a personal landing page about me, single index.html file, inline CSS

steps.question: Which step in this replay is the agent's explore/plan step, done before any file is written?
options: The streamed reasoning text before any tool call
The Write tool call that creates index.html
The tool_result confirming the file was created
The final summary message after the write

steps.md: **The prompt ladder.** Restyle the page three ways — vague, constrained, and planned. Run each branch and compare the diffs: more specific prompts produce more controlled outcomes.

suggestions.text: restyle the page with a dark theme
restyle the page with a dark theme, keep it one file, no frameworks
restyle the page with a dark theme and a new accent color, keep it one file, no frameworks

vague.expectedPrompt: restyle the page with a dark theme

constrained.expectedPrompt: restyle the page with a dark theme, keep it one file, no frameworks

plan-mode.expectedPrompt: restyle the page with a dark theme and a new accent color, keep it one file, no frameworks

steps.question: Comparing the three restyle diffs, which prompt produced the most tightly controlled outcome?
options: The vague prompt
The constrained prompt
The plan-mode prompt (constrained, plus a reviewed plan)

steps.md: **Plan mode.** Ask for a plan before any file changes. You can approve it as-is, or revise it first and watch execution follow the revised plan.

suggestions.text: add a skills section to the page listing 3 example skills, keep it one file
add a skills section to the page listing 3 example skills, keep it one file

approve.expectedPrompt: add a skills section to the page listing 3 example skills, keep it one file

revise.expectedPrompt: add a skills section to the page listing 3 example skills, keep it one file

steps.question: What's the main benefit of revising a plan before approving it, rather than approving immediately?
options: You catch mismatches between what you asked for and what the agent intends to do, before any files change
It makes the agent respond faster
It skips the permission gate entirely
It automatically switches to a cheaper model

steps.md: **Permission modes are the leash.** Run the same change in plan, acceptEdits, and bypassPermissions modes. Notice where each mode pauses for your review — and where it doesn't.

suggestions.text: rewrite the projects section removing the placeholder cards, keep it one file
rewrite the projects section removing the placeholder cards, keep it one file
rewrite the projects section removing the placeholder cards, keep it one file

plan.expectedPrompt: rewrite the projects section removing the placeholder cards, keep it one file

acceptEdits.expectedPrompt: rewrite the projects section removing the placeholder cards, keep it one file

bypass.expectedPrompt: rewrite the projects section removing the placeholder cards, keep it one file

steps.question: Which permission mode pauses for your review before a risky edit is applied?
options: plan mode
acceptEdits
bypassPermissions

steps.md: **Review before you accept.** Two runs add a testimonials section; one plants a bug. Read the diffs in the workspace pane and catch it before it ships.

suggestions.text: add a testimonials section to the page with two example quotes, style it with a CSS class named .testimonial-card defined in the stylesheet
add a testimonials section to the page with two example quotes, reference a CSS class named .testimonial-card in the HTML but do not add a .testimonial-card rule anywhere in the stylesheet
suggestions.description: the change with a planted bug

clean.expectedPrompt: add a testimonials section to the page with two example quotes, style it with a CSS class named .testimonial-card defined in the stylesheet

planted-bug.expectedPrompt: add a testimonials section to the page with two example quotes, reference a CSS class named .testimonial-card in the HTML but do not add a .testimonial-card rule anywhere in the stylesheet

steps.md: **Teach your agent.** The same prompt, with and without a CLAUDE.md conventions file in the workspace. Watch how the conventions change what Claude writes.

suggestions.text: add a call-to-action button to the page using a new highlight color, define the highlight as a CSS custom property
add a call-to-action button to the page using a new highlight color, define the highlight as a CSS custom property
suggestions.description: run with a CLAUDE.md conventions file

without.expectedPrompt: add a call-to-action button to the page using a new highlight color, define the highlight as a CSS custom property

with.expectedPrompt: add a call-to-action button to the page using a new highlight color, define the highlight as a CSS custom property

steps.question: The 'with CLAUDE.md' run named its new CSS variable --gp-highlight instead of --highlight. What caused that?
options: A convention defined in the workspace's CLAUDE.md file
A different prompt text
A different permission mode
Random variation between runs

steps.md: **Cost & models.** Run the same small tweak on Haiku and on Sonnet with the token meter on, and decide which model this task actually needs.

suggestions.text: update the call-to-action button label to say Get in touch, keep everything else the same
update the call-to-action button label to say Get in touch, keep everything else the same

haiku.expectedPrompt: update the call-to-action button label to say Get in touch, keep everything else the same

sonnet.expectedPrompt: update the call-to-action button label to say Get in touch, keep everything else the same

steps.question: For a small, well-specified text tweak like this one, which model is the better cost/quality tradeoff?
options: Haiku — the task is simple and well-scoped
Sonnet — always default to the most capable model
Neither — this needs a human to do it by hand
Cost doesn't matter, model choice is irrelevant
